raw_response:
# SOC 2 Readiness Assessment — P1.1 Privacy Notice
**Orion Cloud Services AB | Q3–Q4 2025**
**Prepared by:** Compliance Auditor
**Scope Period:** July 1, 2025 – December 31, 2025

---

## Assessment Methodology

Evidence reviewed across five documents: Privacy Policy (v5.0), DSAR Log, Extension Register, Incident Response Plan, and Vendor Security Questionnaire Summary. The latter two were flagged as not relevant to P1.1 and were set aside after confirmation. Cross-referencing focused on whether (1) notice commitments stated in the Privacy Policy are operationally honored, and (2) DSAR handling meets the timelines and communication obligations Orion represents to data subjects. Where the DSAR log and extension register provided corroborating evidence of proper process, potential flags were resolved as compliant. Genuine gaps were distinguished from edge cases with proper justification.

---

## Findings

---

### Finding 1: Active DSAR With No Communication and No Extension Filed

**Title:** DSAR-2025-017 — Delayed Response Without Data Subject Notification or Extension

**Document Reference:**
- DSAR Log — Row DSAR-2025-017 (Lena Bergstrom, received October 15, 2025)
- Privacy Policy — Section 7 (Exercising Your Rights)
- GDPR Article 12(3) by reference

**Detailed Observation:**

This is the most significant finding in the assessment. DSAR-2025-017 was received on October 15, 2025. As of the assessment date, the status is "In Progress" with no response date recorded, no extension filed, and — critically — the notes field explicitly states: *"No extension filed. No communication sent to data subject regarding delay."*

The Privacy Policy (Section 7) commits to responding within **30 calendar days** of receipt. That deadline fell on or around **November 14, 2025**. The assessment period extends to December 31, 2025, meaning this request has been open for approximately **77 days** with no response and no proactive communication to the data subject.

The stated reason for delay is a pending legal review of whether internal audit trails constitute personal data under Article 15. While legal uncertainty is a legitimate operational challenge, GDPR Article 12(3) provides a clear mechanism to address complexity: file an extension and notify the data subject within the original 30-day window. Orion used this mechanism correctly in six other instances (EXT-2025-001 through EXT-2025-006). The procedure exists, is well-established internally, and was not applied here.

The gap is not the legal question itself — it is the complete absence of communication with the data subject and the failure to invoke the extension procedure before the deadline elapsed.

**Why This Matters for P1.1:**
P1.1 requires that the entity provide notice consistent with its stated privacy practices. Orion's Privacy Policy explicitly represents a 30-day response commitment to data subjects. Failing to honor that commitment — and failing to notify the data subject of the delay — means the operational reality does not match the notice provided. A data subject reading Section 7 of Orion's Privacy Policy and submitting a request would have a reasonable expectation of a response or at minimum a communication by November 14, 2025. Neither occurred.

**Severity: High**

> **Rationale:** This is a direct, documented, in-period failure with no mitigating factors. The extension mechanism was available and proven. The data subject received no communication for over 45+ days past the stated deadline. This creates regulatory exposure under GDPR Article 12 and directly contradicts the notice commitment in Section 7 of the Privacy Policy. It is not Critical only because it involves a single request rather than a systemic breakdown in the DSAR process, and because all other in-scope DSARs were handled properly.

---

### Finding 2: Retention Schedule Referenced But Not Publicly Accessible

**Title:** Data Retention Periods Not Disclosed in Privacy Notice

**Document Reference:**
- Privacy Policy — Section 8 (Data Retention)

**Detailed Observation:**

Section 8 states: *"Specific retention periods by data category are documented in our internal Data Retention Schedule, available upon request."*

Two issues arise from this language:

First, characterizing the schedule as an "internal" document while simultaneously stating it is "available upon request" creates an ambiguity. Data subjects reading the Privacy Policy cannot determine retention periods for their data without submitting a separate inquiry. GDPR Article 13(2)(a) requires that retention periods — or at minimum the criteria used to determine them — be provided as part of the privacy notice itself, not withheld pending a request.

Second, only a single aggregate retention rule is disclosed in the policy body: *"the duration of your account plus 24 months after closure."* This applies uniformly across all data categories listed in Section 3 (Account Data, Usage Data, Communication Data, Analytics Data, Payment Data), which is unlikely to reflect actual practice. For example, payment-related data held by Stripe and communication data in support tickets would typically carry different retention periods driven by financial regulation and business need respectively. A single blanket retention period across all categories suggests the disclosed rule is either incomplete or not operationally accurate.

**Why This Matters for P1.1:**
P1.1 requires the privacy notice to meet the entity's objectives related to privacy. If the notice understates or obscures how long data is held, data subjects cannot make informed decisions and the notice fails its transparency purpose. Directing data subjects to request an internal document rather than disclosing retention periods in the notice itself is inconsistent with the spirit of transparency that P1.1 and GDPR Article 13 require.

**Severity: Medium**

> **Rationale:** The single blanket retention statement and the internal document reference are a structural deficiency in the notice rather than an active operational harm. No data subject has been demonstrably misled in the evidence provided. However, this is a recurring point of scrutiny in GDPR audits and creates audit risk if examined by a supervisory authority. It warrants remediation before a SOC 2 audit opinion is issued.

---

### Finding 3: Legal Basis for Processing Not Disclosed in Privacy Notice

**Title:** Lawful Bases for Processing Absent from Privacy Policy

**Document Reference:**
- Privacy Policy — Section 4 (How We Use Your Information)

**Detailed Observation:**

Section 4 describes six processing purposes (platform provision, transaction processing, customer support, analytics, security, legal compliance) but does not identify the lawful basis under GDPR Article 6 for any of them. GDPR Article 13(1)(c) requires that the legal basis for each processing activity be included in the privacy notice provided to data subjects.

For a B2B SaaS platform, the applicable bases will typically vary across purposes — for example, **contract performance** (Article 6(1)(b)) for account management and service delivery; **legitimate interests** (Article 6(1)(f)) for fraud detection, security monitoring, and analytics; **legal obligation** (Article 6(1)(c)) for tax and regulatory record-keeping; and **consent** (Article 6(1)(a)) for session recordings (referenced in Section 3). The current policy omits this mapping entirely.

This is particularly notable because Section 7 advises data subjects they have a right to object to processing — a right that is specifically tied to processing carried out under legitimate interests. Without knowing which activities rely on that basis, a data subject cannot meaningfully exercise the right to object as described in the same section.

**Why This Matters for P1.1:**
The notice as written gives data subjects an incomplete picture of the legal grounds on which their data is processed. This undermines the notice's ability to support informed consent decisions, the right to object, and rights withdrawal, all of which are referenced in Section 7. The gap between what the policy promises in Section 7 and what Section 4 actually discloses creates an internal inconsistency within the notice itself.

**Severity: Medium**

> **Rationale:** Omission of legal bases is a known and common GDPR enforcement area. It is a structural gap in the notice document rather than an operational failure. The severity is Medium rather than High because Orion's B2B context (enterprise customers who have their own DPAs) somewhat reduces the population of directly affected data subjects, and no evidence suggests data subjects have been actively harmed by the omission during the review period.

---

### Finding 4: Cookie and Tracking Technology Disclosures Absent

**Title:** No Cookie Policy or Tracking Technology Disclosure Referenced in Privacy Policy

**Document Reference:**
- Privacy Policy — Section 3 (Information We Collect); General

**Detailed Observation:**

Section 3 references session recordings (with consent), IP addresses, browser and device information, and click path analytics. These activities typically involve cookies, tracking pixels, or similar technologies governed by the ePrivacy Directive and, in Sweden, the Swedish Electronic Communications Act (LEK).

The Privacy Policy contains no mention of cookies, no link to a separate cookie policy, no description of categories of cookies used (strictly necessary, functional, analytics, marketing), and no explanation of how consent for tracking technologies is obtained or withdrawn. The reference to "session recordings (with consent)" in Section 3 is the only acknowledgment that consent is required for any data collection activity, and it provides no mechanism or detail.

For a platform with a public-facing website (www.orioncloud.eu) and an enterprise SaaS product accessed via browser, the absence of any tracking technology disclosure in the privacy notice is a material gap.

**Why This Matters for P1.1:**
P1.1 requires that the notice cover all personal information collected. If data is being collected via tracking technologies that are not disclosed, the notice is factually incomplete. Data subjects visiting the website or using the platform have no way to understand or control this data collection based on the current notice.

**Severity: Medium**

> **Rationale:** This is a structural omission in the notice. The severity is not elevated to High because the evidence does not confirm the volume or nature of tracking in use — it is possible that tracking is minimal and limited to technically necessary functions. However, the absence of any disclosure is itself the gap, and it requires remediation regardless of the actual tracking footprint.

---

## Summary Table

| # | Finding Title | Document Section | Severity |
|---|---------------|-----------------|----------|
| 1 | DSAR-2025-017 — Delayed Response Without Data Subject Notification or Extension | DSAR Log / Privacy Policy §7 | **High** |
| 2 | Data Retention Periods Not Disclosed in Privacy Notice | Privacy Policy §8 | **Medium** |
| 3 | Lawful Bases for Processing Absent from Privacy Policy | Privacy Policy §4 | **Medium** |
| 4 | No Cookie Policy or Tracking Technology Disclosure | Privacy Policy §3 | **Medium** |

---

## Items Reviewed and Confirmed Compliant

The following were examined as potential flags and resolved as compliant based on corroborating evidence:

| Item | Resolution |
|------|-----------|
| Six DSAR extensions filed in 2025 | All six extensions were DPO-approved, data subjects were notified within one business day of approval, all responses were delivered on or before extended deadlines, and complexity justifications were documented. The extension process is well-controlled and functioning. |
| DSAR response times across Q3–Q4 2025 (excluding DSAR-2025-017) | All 12 completed DSARs in scope were responded to within 30 days (range: 4–27 days) or within an approved extended deadline. No other overdue responses were identified. |
| DPO identity and contact information | Dr. Anna Kowalski is named, her address, email, and phone are published, and she is actively approving extensions throughout the log — indicating the disclosed contact is operational. |
| International transfer mechanism | SCCs and adequacy decisions are cited in §6. Infrastructure is primarily EEA-based (AWS eu-north-1). Consistent with disclosed vendor footprint. |
| Policy update notification commitment | Section 10 commits to 30-day advance notice via email for material changes. No evidence of a policy update in the review period that would trigger this obligation; no testing of this control was possible from available evidence, but the commitment is clearly stated. |

---

## Recommended Remediation Actions

| Priority | Action |
|----------|--------|
| **Immediate** | Resolve DSAR-2025-017: Send interim communication to Lena Bergstrom acknowledging the delay, provide an estimated response date, and retroactively document the legal review rationale. Consider whether an Article 12(3) extension can still be logged. Review whether legal counsel's scope determination can be expedited. |
| **Short-term (30–60 days)** | Amend Privacy Policy Section 4 to map each processing purpose to its Article 6 lawful basis. |
| **Short-term (30–60 days)** | Amend Privacy Policy Section 8 to include category-specific retention periods or at minimum the criteria used to determine them. Retire the "internal document available upon request" language. |
| **Short-term (60–90 days)** | Add a cookie and tracking technology disclosure to the Privacy Policy or publish a standalone Cookie Policy linked from the main policy and website footer. |
| **Process** | Implement a DSAR aging alert — automated notification to the DPO when a DSAR reaches Day 20 without a response or filed extension — to prevent recurrence of the DSAR-2025-017 situation. |
